6 LEGAL STANDARD 
7 I. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) 
8 Federal Rule of Civil Procedure ("Rule") 12(b)(6) permits dismissal for failure to 
9 state a claim upon which relief can be granted. Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6). Rule 8(a)(2) 
10 states that a pleading must contain "a short and plain statement of the claim showing that 
11 the pleader is entitled to relief." Fed. R. Civ. P. 8(a)(2). A plaintiff is not required to 
12 provide "detailed factual allegations," but the plaintiff must plead sufficient facts that, if 
13 accepted as true, "raise a right to relief above the speculative level." Bell Atlantic Corp. v. 
14 Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 545 (2007). A complaint will survive a motion to dismiss when 
15 it contains enough facts to "state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face." Ashcroft v. 
16 Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (quoting Twombly, 550 U.S. at 570). A claim is plausible 
17 when the factual allegations permit "the court to draw the reasonable inference that the 
18 defendant is liable for the misconduct charged." Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678. The Court is not 
19 required to accept as true "allegations that are merely conclusory, unwarranted 
20 deductions of fact, or unreasonable inferences." In re Gilead Scis. Sec. Litig., 536 F.3d 
21 1049, 1055 (9th Cir. 2008). 
22 DISCUSSION 
23 Defendant Young seeks dismissal of the SAC on the following grounds: (1) this 
24 Court lacks personal jurisdiction over her; (2) venue in the Southern District of California 
25 is improper; and (3) Plaintiff fails to state a claim because Plaintiff's ERISA claim as to 
26 Young is barred by both collateral estoppel and collateral attack, and the Court should not 
27 
1 exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the state law claims. ECF No. 97 at 1. 
2 Alternatively, Young seeks transfer of the entire matter to the Northern District of Texas. 
3 ECF No. 97 at 1. 
4 Defendants Ligand and the 401(k) Plan seek dismissal on the following grounds: 
5 (1) the entire action is barred as an impermissible collateral attack on the Texas orders; 
6 (2) res judicata and issue preclusion bar Plaintiff's claims; (3) Plaintiff's fourth cause of 
7 action fails to plead causation and Plaintiff lacks standing to sue for a procedural 
8 violation of ERISA; (4) Plaintiff lacks standing to bring his second and third causes of 
9 action; (5) Plaintiff fails to plead a claim as to count one because the transfer to Young 
10 was not a "distribution" from Plaintiff's 401(k) account; (6) Plaintiff fails to state a claim 
11 as to counts ten and eleven; and (7) Plaintiff's retaliation claim is barred by the statute of 
12 limitations and lacks merit. ECF No. 100. Alternatively, Defendants join Young's motion 
13 to transfer the entire matter to the Northern District of Texas. ECF No. 100 at 23. 
14 I. Timeliness 
15 Plaintiff alleges that both Motions to Dismiss should be denied as untimely. ECF 
16 No. 107 at 7; ECF No. 106 at 7. Plaintiff filed his SAC on May 25, 2022. ECF No. 92. 
17 Under Rule 15, a response to an amended pleading is due within 14 days. Fed. R. Civ. P. 
18 15. Thus, Plaintiff states the deadline for Defendants to file their Motions to Dismiss was 
19 June 8, 2022. Defendant Young filed a Motion to Dismiss on June 14, 2022, ECF No. 97, 
20 and Defendant Ligand filed a Motion to Dismiss on June 15, 2022, ECF No. 100. 
21 The Ninth Circuit states that a negligent failure to respond is excusable if the party 
22 offers a credible, good faith explanation for the untimeliness that "negat[es] any intention 
23 to take advantage of the opposing party, interfere with judicial decisionmaking, or 
24 otherwise manipulate the legal process." TCI Group Life Ins. Plan v. Knoebber, 244 F.3d 
25 691, 697 (9th Cir. 2001). Here, Defendants offer a good faith explanation that the alleged 
26 untimeliness was due to a stipulation among the Parties that responsive pleadings were 
27 
1 not due until 21 days after lifting the stay. ECF No. 109. The Court finds that this is a 
2 good faith explanation and that the untimeliness in no way interfered with the judicial 
3 process or took advantage of Plaintiff. Thus, the Court does not deny the Motions on this 
4 basis alone. 
5 II. Transfer 
6 At the outset, the Court denies transfer to the Northern District of Texas. 28 U.S.C. 
7 § 1404(a) governs a motion to transfer. Transfer is warranted if: (1) the action could have 
8 originally been brought in the transferee court; and (2) convenience of the parties and 
9 witnesses in the interest of justice favors transfer. See Hatch v. Reliance Ins. Co., 758 
10 F.2d 409, 414 (9th Cir. 1985). All Parties agree that this matter could have originally 
11 been brought in the Northern District of Texas, therefore the Court does not address this 
12 prong. 
13 The second prong weighs multiple factors, including: (1) the convenience of the 
14 parties and witnesses; (2) the location where the relevant agreements were negotiated and 
15 executed; (3) the state that is most familiar with the governing law; (4) the plaintiff's 
16 forum choice; (5) the respective parties' contacts with the forum; (6) the contacts relating 
17 to the plaintiff's cause of action in the chosen forum; (7) the difference in litigation costs 
18 between the two forums; (8) the availability of the compulsory process to compel 
19 attendance of unwilling non-party witnesses; and (9) the ease of access to sources of 
20 proof. Jones v. GNC Franchising, Inc., 211 F.3d 495, 598 (9th Cir. 2000). One factor is 
21 not dispositive, and a district court has broad discretion to decide motions for transfer on 
22 an individualized basis. See Ctr. For Biological Diversity v. Kempthorne, No. 08-1339, 
23 2008 WL 4543043, at *2 (N.D. Cal. Oct. 10, 2008 (citing Stewart Org., Inc. v. Ricoh 
24 Corp., 487 U.S. 22, 29 (1988)). For the following reasons, the Court finds transfer is not 
25 appropriate in this case. 
26 
27

18 III. Collateral Estoppel / Res Judicata 
19 Both Defendants argue that Plaintiff's claims are barred by res judicata and 
20 collateral estoppel. ECF No. 100 at 11; ECF No. 97 at 33. Specifically, Defendants argue 
21 that underlying each of Plaintiff's claims is the validity of the 401(k) QDRO and Stock 
22 DRO, and that the Texas courts have already decided these documents are valid. To 
23 determine the preclusive effect of a state court judgment, federal courts must look to the 
24 state's preclusion law. Palomar Mobilehome Park Ass'n v. City of San Marcos, 989 F.2d 
25 362, 364 (9th Cir. 1993). 
26 / 
27 
1 A. Res Judicata 
2 Res judicata, or claim preclusion, under Texas law, bars the litigation of claims 
3 that were litigated or should have been litigated in an earlier suit. Hammervold v. Blank, 3 
4 F.4th 803, 808 (5th Cir. 2021). Under Texas law, "a judgment in an earlier suit precludes 
5 a second action by the parties and their privies not only on matters actually litigated, but 
6 also on causes of action or defenses which arise out of the same subject matter and which 
7 might have been litigated in the first suit." Getty Oil Co. v. Ins. Co. of N. Am., 845 
8 S.W.2d 794, 798 (Tex. 1992). Claim preclusion requires proof of: (1) a prior final 
9 judgment on the merits by a court of competent jurisdiction; (2) the identical identity of 
10 parties or those in privity with them; and (3) the second action is based on the same 
11 claims as were raised or could have been raised in the first action. Amstadt v. U.S. Brass 
12 Corp., 919 S.W.2d 644, 652 (Tex. 1996). To determine if the third element is satisfied, 
13 the court should apply the "transactional test, which requires that the two actions be 
14 based on the same nucleus of operative facts." Stanford v. Kelley, No. 4:21-cv-388, 2022 
15 WL 670929, at *5 (E.D. Tex. Feb. 12, 2022). If these three requirements are met, "res 
16 judicata prohibits either party from raising any claim or defense in the later action that 
17 was or could have been raised in support of or in opposition to the cause of action 
18 asserted in the prior action." Id. (quoting Trevino v. Bank of N.Y. Mellon, No. 1:19-cv-

2 Under Texas law, collateral estoppel requires finding that: "‘(1) the facts sought to 
3 be litigated in the second action were fully and fairly litigated in the prior action; (2)

5 as adversaries in the first action.'" Hammervold, 3 F.4th at 810 (quoting In re Schwager, 
6 121 F.3d 177, 181 (5th Cir. 1997)). "The issue in the first action [must] be identical to the

8 the facts and ‘legal standard used to assess them'" are identical. Hammervold, 3 F.4th at 
9 810 (quoting Brister v. A.W.I., Inc., 946 F.2d 350, 354 n.1 (5th Cir. 1991)). If a party is 
10 collaterally estopped from proving a necessary element of their claim because the 
11 identical issue was decided in previous litigation, the entire claim can be dismissed. Id. at 
12 810.

17 Fullerton, 118 F.3d 374, 377 (5th Cir. 1997) (quoting Sysco Food Services, Inc. v. 
18 Trapnell, 890 S.W.2d 796, 801 (Tex. 1994)). Plaintiff was a party in the prior actions.

18 IV. Collateral Attack 
19 Collateral attack "precludes litigants from collaterally attacking the judgments of 
20 other courts." Rein v. Providian Fin. Corp., 270 F.3d 895, 902 (9th Cir. 2001) (citing 
21 Celotex Corp. v. Edwards, 514 U.S. 300, 313 (1995)). Collateral attack is different from 
22 collateral estoppel or res judicata. See Trahan v. Superior Oil Co., 700 F.2d 1004, 1019 
23 (5th Cir. 1983). "‘It is for the court of first instance to determine the question of the 
24 validity of the law, and until its decision is reversed for error by orderly review, either by 
25 itself or by a higher court, its orders based on its decision are to be respected.'" Walker v. 
26 Birmingham, 388 U.S. 307, 314 (1967) (quoting Howat v. Kansas, 258 U.S. 181, 189–
27 
1 190 (1922)). Collateral attack bars consideration of a claim when a court must "re-
2 examine and decide a question which has been finally determined." City of Tacoma v. 
3 Taxpayers of Tacoma, 357 U.S. 320, 334 (1958). The prior judgment must actually 
4 address the specific issue and decide that issue for the collateral attack doctrine to apply. 
5 See Rein, 270 F.3d at 902 (finding the collateral attack doctrine does not apply because 
6 the claims were not addressed by a prior order or judgment). 
7 Collateral attack will apply when a Plaintiff can prevail on their later claims "only 
8 by proving that the [prior decision] was improper." Gilbert v. Ben-Asher, 900 F.2d 1407, 
9 1411 (9th Cir. 1990) (finding the current lawsuit was a "collateral attack on the 
10 administrative decision revoking the license under the guise of a complaint sounding in 
11 tort"). A court must ask if the "pivotal issue has already been litigated and decided 
12 against [the Plaintiff]." Id.

14 entire SAC are barred by the collateral attack doctrine. Given that the Court has applied

17 consider Defendant Ligand's collateral attack argument here as to the surviving claims

24 retaliation under ERISA did not exist at the time of the Texas proceedings and as such 
25 could not have been litigated. ECF No. 106 at 7.

5 V. Kennedy and Ligand's Fiduciary Duty Under ERISA 
6 Although Plaintiff's second and third causes of action are dismissed as barred by 
7 collateral estoppel, these causes of action can alternatively be dismissed under Kennedy v. 
8 Plan Administrator for DuPont Savings and Investment Plan, 555 U.S. 285 (2009). 
9 ERISA does not require, or even permit, plan administrators to perform a detailed 
10 investigation of the underlying merits of a QDRO. See Blue v. UAL Corp., 160 F.3d 383, 
11 385 (7th Cir. 1998). In Kennedy, the Supreme Court stated that "a plan administrator who 
12 enforces a QDRO must be said to enforce plan documents, not ignore them. . . . [A] 
13 QDRO enquiry is relatively discrete, given the specific and objective criteria for a 
14 domestic relations order that qualifies as a QDRO, . . . [the requirements] amount to a 
15 statutory checklist working to ‘spare [an administrator] from litigation-fomenting 
16 ambiguities.'" Kennedy v. Plan Adm'r for DuPont Sav. & Inv. Plan, 555 U.S. 285, 301-
17 02 (2009) (quoting Metropolitan Life Ins. Co. v. Wheaton, 42 F.3d 1080, 1084 (7th Cir. 
18 1994)). A plan administrator is not required to determine questions of law or resolve 
19 factual disputes. Id. "[U]nder ERISA, the pension plan must pay the bearer of a DRO if it 
20 determines that the order is a proper QDRO, without further inquiry; compliance with a 
21 QDRO is obligatory.'" Trs. of the Dirs. Guild of America-Producer Pension Benefits 
22 Plans v. Tise, 234 F.3d 415, 424 (9th Cir. 2000) (citing 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(A) ("Each 
23 pension plan shall provide for the payment of benefits in accordance with the applicable 
24 requirements of any qualified domestic relations order.")). 
25 Kennedy cites to 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(C) and (D) as the "statutory checklist" 
26 plan administrators must follow. Kennedy, 555 U.S. at 302. Specifically, ERISA requires 
27 
1 the following to qualify a DRO: (1) the name and mailing address of the participant and 
2 alternate payee; (2) the amount or percentage of participant's benefits to be paid to each 
3 alternate payee; (3) the number of payments or period to which such order applies; (4) 
4 each plan to which the order applies; (5) the DRO must not require the plan to provide for 
5 a benefit not otherwise provided under the plan; (6) the DRO must not require the plan to 
6 provide increased benefits; and (7) the DRO must not require the payment of benefits to 
7 an alternate payee which are required to be paid to another alternate payee under a prior 
8 QDRO. 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(C), (D). Thus, when a QDRO meets these statutory 
9 criteria, a plan administrator must comply with the QDRO without further inquiry into 
10 whether the QDRO complies with state laws or suffers from any other defect. See Gray v. 
11 I.B.E.W. Local 332 Pension Tr., No. C09-3782 HRL, 2010 WL 3893590, at *3 (N.D. Cal. 
12 Sept. 30, 2010), aff'd, 495 F. App'x 831 (9th Cir. 2012). 
13 The Court previously found that Ligand sufficiently demonstrated it satisfied the 
14 statutory requirements. See ECF No. 100 at 13; ECF No. 64 at 22; ECF No. 49 at 30-31. 
15 The Court again finds that the 401(k) QDRO meets this checklist. The 401(k) QDRO 
16 states the names and mailing addresses of Plaintiff and Defendant Young; it states that 
17 100% of the benefits should be paid to Young; and it states that the Order applies to 
18 Plaintiff's 401(k) Plan. At the hearing, Plaintiff argued that the third prong was not 
19 satisfied—the 401(k) QDRO did not specify "the number of payments or period to which 
20 such order applies." 
21 The Court disagrees and finds that the 401(k) QDRO does meet this requirement. 
22 The 401(k) QDRO clearly states that "Alternate Payee shall be paid Alternate Payee's 
23 benefits as soon as administratively feasible following the date this Order is approved as 
24 a Qualified Domestic Relations Order by the Plan Administrator and at the earliest date 
25 permitted under the terms of the Plan." Young Exhibit 6. Courts have found that similar 
26 language meets the 29 U.S.C. § 1056(d)(3)(C)(iii) requirement. See e.g., In re Williams,

9 Defendant Ligand argues this claim must be dismissed because Plaintiff has not 
10 pled causation, and Plaintiff has not suffered a concrete injury-in-fact. ECF No. 100 at 
11 15-17. Ligand argues Article III does not grant standing for "bare procedural violations." 
12 Id. The Court disagrees. 
13 1. Standing 
14 To establish standing under ERISA, an individual must show both that they have 
15 standing under the statute and that they meet Article III standing requirements. Wells v. 
16 California Physicians' Serv., No. C05-01229 CRB, 2007 WL 926490, at *3 (N.D. Cal. 
17 Mar. 26, 2007). To have standing under ERISA, a Plaintiff must show that they are a plan 
18 participant. Poore v. Simpson Paper Co., 566 F.3d 922, 925 (9th Cir. 2009). A plan 
19 participant is "any employee or former employee of an employer . . . who is or may 
20 become eligible to receive a benefit of any type from an employee benefit plan which 
21 covers employees of such employer." 29 U.S.C. § 1002(7). There appears to be no 
22 dispute that Plaintiff has standing under the ERISA statue to bring the fourth cause of 
23 action. 
24 To establish Article III standing, a Plaintiff must show: (1) they have suffered an 
25 injury-in-fact; (2) the injury is fairly traceable to the challenged conduct of the defendant; 
26 and (3) the injury is likely to be redressed by a favorable judicial decision. Spokeo, Inc. v. 
27 
1 Robins, 578 U.S. 330, 338 (2016). As an initial matter, the Court notes that the Order 
2 granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss the FAC was published prior to the Supreme 
3 Court's most recent ruling on Article III standing in TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, 141 S. 
4 Ct. 2190 (2021). As such, the analysis in this Order differs from the standing analysis in 
5 the Order dismissing the FAC. 
6 An injury-in-fact "must be concrete, that is, real, and not abstract." TransUnion 
7 LLC v. Ramirez, 141 S. Ct. 2190, 2204 (2021). "‘[C]oncrete' is not . . . necessarily 
8 synonymous with ‘tangible.'" Spokeo, 578 U.S. at 340; see also Patel v. Facebook, Inc., 
9 932 F.3d 1264, 1270 (9th Cir. 2019). Intangible injuries can be concrete. TransUnion, 
10 141 S. Ct. at 2204. However, a plaintiff does not automatically satisfy the injury-in-fact 
11 requirement when a statute grants an individual a statutory right, and a statutory 
12 obligation does not "relieve courts of their responsibility to independently decide whether 
13 a plaintiff has suffered a concrete harm under Article III." Id. at 2205. 
14 Here, Plaintiff alleges an informational injury in the sense that Ligand failed to 
15 notify Plaintiff of their policy for determining the qualified status of a DRO and Ligand's 
16 failure to notify Plaintiff that they had qualified the 401(k) QDRO. Although the Court in 
17 TransUnion put forth a historical inquiry test to determine when statutory violations 
18 constitute concrete injury for the purposes of Article III standing, TransUnion leaves 
19 untouched, and in effect reaffirms, the possibility of a purely informational injury as in 
20 Federal Elections Commission v. Akins, 524 U.S. 11 (1998), provided Plaintiff alleges he 
21 has suffered some harm as a result of the informational injury. TransUnion, 141 S. Ct. at 
22 2214 (stating that an "asserted informational injury that causes no adverse effects cannot 
23 satisfy Article III"). An informational injury can constitute concrete harm under Article 
24 III so long as Plaintiff alleges actual harm resulting from the informational injury. 
25 Here, Plaintiff alleges that Ligand's failure to provide him information required 
26 under ERISA caused him harm because he did not know the "time limits" that were set 
27 
1 by the plan administrator for making determinations, and that if he had this information, 
2 he would have better been able to "evaluate his options in contesting the validity of the 
3 orders in state court versus other venues." SAC ¶ 120. He also was unable to "evaluate 
4 the proper court to apply to for relief or whether Ligand had internal administrative 
5 procedures available." SAC ¶ 121. Thus, Plaintiff has identified "downstream 
6 consequences" from the informational injury he has suffered under ERISA. TransUnion, 
7 141 S. Ct. at 2214 (quoting Trichell v. Midland Credit Mgmt., Inc., 964 F.3d 990, 1004 
8 (11th Cir. 2020)). Plaintiff has pled facts that support more than a "bare procedural 
9 violation." Spokeo, 578 U.S. at 341. 
10 2. Causation 
11 Ligand also argues Plaintiff fails to plead the fourth cause of action because he 
12 "does not allege causation—specifically, how he would have obtained a better outcome 
13 had he been provided with a copy of the Plan's procedures for determining the qualified 
14 status of domestic relations orders." ECF No. 100-1 at 25 (emphasis in original). The 
15 Court disagrees for the reasons stated in the previous section. Plaintiff's Second 
16 Amended Complaint clearly alleges that failure to receive a copy of the Plan's procedures 
17 caused him harm in the sense that he was not able to best evaluate his options for how to 
18 proceed with challenging the orders. Thus, Plaintiff has properly pled standing and 
19 causation to support the fourth cause of action. 
20 C. Ninth Cause of Action 
21 Plaintiff's ninth cause of action alleges that Defendant Young breached a 
22 protective order by using confidential information acquired from Ligand to obtain the 
23 QDRO and DRO. SAC ¶¶ 170, 172. Defendant Young states that this allegation fails as a 
24 matter of law because the terms of the protective order itself permit the alleged breaches. 
25 ECF No. 97 at 35 (citing Young Exhibit 21). Plaintiff failed to oppose Defendant 
26 
27 
1 Young's challenge, and thus the Court finds Plaintiff concedes this argument and that the 
2 ninth cause of action must be DISMISSED. 
3 D. Twelfth Cause of Action 
4 Plaintiff's twelfth cause of action alleges Ligand violated ERISA's anti-retaliation 
5 provision, § 510, when it terminated his employment in alleged retaliation for his 
6 exercise of rights under ERISA. SAC ¶ 95. To establish a claim under this provision, 
7 Plaintiff must show that: (1) he participated in a statutorily protected activity; (2) an 
8 adverse employment action was taken against him; and (3) a causal connection existed 
9 between the two. Kimbro v. Atl. Richfield Co., 889 F.2d 869, 881 (9th Cir. 1989). 
10 Defendant Ligand argues the twelfth cause of action is barred by the statute of 
11 limitations. ECF No. 100 at 22. Because § 510 of ERISA does not contain its own statute 
12 of limitations, the Court must apply the most analogous state law statute of limitations 
13 period. Here, the most analogous statute of limitations is the two-year statute of 
14 limitations for wrongful termination. See Karamsetty v. Wells Fargo & Co., 967 F. Supp. 
15 2d 1305, 1321 (N.D. Cal. 2013). At the motion to dismiss stage, "a complaint cannot be 
16 dismissed unless it appears beyond doubt that the plaintiff can prove no set of facts that 
17 would establish the timeliness of the claim.'" Hernandez v. City of El Monte, 138 F.3d 
18 393, 402 (9th Cir. 1998) (quoting Supermail Cargo, Inc. v. United States, 68 F.3d 1204, 
19 1207 (9th Cir. 1995)). The Court agrees with Plaintiff that it is not apparent the statute of 
20 limitations has run because the allegedly retaliatory conduct started in January 2019 and 
21 continued until he was terminated in June 2021. ECF No. 106 at 22. The SAC was filed 
22 in May 2022. ECF No. 92. It is not apparent from the face of Plaintiff's SAC that there is 
23 no set of facts to establish the timeliness of his claim. 
24 Further, the Court finds Plaintiff has adequately pled his retaliation claim. Plaintiff 
25 states his protected activity was bringing an action under ERISA to correct alleged 
26 ERISA violations. SAC ¶ 195. Plaintiff alleges he suffered an adverse employment action 
27 
1 in the form of salary reduction and termination. SAC ¶¶ 199-200, 202. Last, Plaintiff 
2 pleads facts to show a potential causal link—the retaliatory action occurred after Plaintiff 
3 commenced this action. Thus, Plaintiff has stated a claim under ERISA's anti-retaliation 
4 provision. 
5 VII. Personal Jurisdiction as to Young 
6 Defendant Young also raises a challenge based upon lack of personal jurisdiction. 
7 Because the Court has dismissed all claims against Defendant Young, this challenge is 
8 moot. 
9 VIII. Motion to Seal 
10 Young filed a motion to file documents under seal accompanying her Motion to 
11 Dismiss the SAC. ECF No. 112. Young seeks to file under seal Exhibits 1-11 and 15-20. 
12 ECF No. 112. To support her argument, Young cites the prior sealing order by the Texas 
13 state court which oversaw the case for which these documents were filed. ECF No. 112. 
14 The Court has previously granted Young's motions to seal documents filed in Texas state 
15 court and San Diego superior court, and those documents are identical to the Exhibits in 
16 the present motion. ECF No. 64. Given the prior sealing order by the Texas state court 
17 and for reasons similar to those stated in the Court's prior Order granting motions to seal, 
18 the Court GRANTS Young's motion to file Exhibits 1-11 and 15-20 under seal. 
19 CONCLUSION 
20 Based on the above reasons, the Court GRANTS Defendant Young's Motion to 
21 Dismiss in full. The Court GRANTS in part and DENIES in part Defendant Ligand's 
22 Motion to Dismiss. Specifically, the second, third, fifth, sixth, seventh, eighth, ninth, 
23 tenth, and eleventh causes of actions are DISMISSED WITH PREJUDICE. Only 
24 Plaintiff's first, fourth, and twelfth causes of action as to Defendant Ligand and the 
25 401(k) Plan remain.
